items.q: Which f-number lets in the most light?
items.correct: The smallest f-number (e.g. f/1.8) — it is the widest aperture.
items.misconception: The biggest f-number (e.g. f/22), because bigger means more.
items.why: The inverted f-stop scale misleads students; the number is a ratio, so smaller numbers mean wider openings.

items.q: To freeze a sprinter mid-stride you should use:
items.correct: A fast shutter speed such as 1/1000 s.
items.misconception: A slow shutter speed, to give the camera more time to capture them.
items.why: Students reason 'more time = better capture', but a longer exposure records the movement as blur.

items.q: What is the downside of a very high ISO?
items.correct: Digital noise and grain that reduce image quality.
items.misconception: Nothing — higher ISO is always better in the dark.
items.why: Students see ISO as free light, missing the trade-off of noise and lost detail at high sensitivity.

items.q: Which f-stop gives the widest aperture?
items.why: It's f/1.8. “f/8” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which setting is most likely to freeze a fast-moving subject?
items.why: Correct answer: Shutter speed of 1/1000. “Shutter speed of 1/15” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many sides does the exposure triangle have?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
